path.name: Workforce Systems Programming
path.description: A live workforce systems course where code-familiar builders learn to architect and operate a Screeps colony by reading the environment, composing game components, automating decisions, and preparing for capture-the-flag competition.
path.targetAudience: High school programmers, college CS students, junior software engineers, career switchers, and tech-interested builders who already understand code basics and want to become more sophisticated systems thinkers.

programs.name: AI Systems Programming Lab
programs.description: A six-week live cohort for builders who already know code basics and want to design smarter systems. You study the Screeps environment, compose colony components, automate decisions, and spend the final week battling your code in capture-the-flag.
programs.learningOutcomes: Read an unfamiliar software environment, model available components, design colony roles, manage state, automate resource decisions, debug live behavior, use Git and Codex responsibly, prepare tournament strategy, and explain why the system behaves the way it does.
programs.projectSummary: A Screeps colony system you design that can harvest energy, spawn creeps, assign roles, persist memory, recover from failures, respond to hostile pressure, adapt to resource constraints, and compete under capture-the-flag rules.
programs.portfolioArtifact: Your Git repository with Screeps bot code, commits, README notes, screenshots or match clips, architecture notes, tournament-week strategy notes, and a final reflection on how your system performed against other colonies.
programs.liveSchedule: Tuesdays and Thursdays, 6:30 PM-8:30 PM Eastern
programs.cohortNote: New cohorts run every so often. The next cohort opens Monday, August 3, 2026. Live sessions begin Tuesday, August 4, 2026. Phase 2 league rollout follows after cohort tournaments are running.

sessions.name: Session 1: Screeps Environment and System Map
sessions.objectives: Set up Screeps, connect the development workflow, and read the colony as an environment with resources, components, actions, state, threats, and feedback.
sessions.liveActivity: Create the Screeps account or local environment, join the cohort server, create the first room, place the spawn, and map the sources, controller, creeps, memory, terrain, and available actions.
sessions.homework: Capture a room screenshot and label the components, constraints, signals, and decisions the colony needs to understand.

sessions.name: Session 2: Colony State and Resource Signals
sessions.objectives: Model the state and signals the colony needs to make better decisions across ticks.
sessions.liveActivity: Read room, creep, source, spawn, and controller data, then decide which values should drive harvesting, upgrading, and spawning behavior.
sessions.homework: Add three meaningful state signals to the Screeps bot and explain how each one changes a colony decision.

sessions.name: Session 3: Components and Repeatable Creep Behavior
sessions.objectives: Turn game actions into reusable behavior components that make the colony easier to reason about and extend.
sessions.liveActivity: Refactor harvesting, transferring, and upgrading actions into named behavior components and use Codex to pressure-test the design.
sessions.homework: Create two Screeps behavior components and write notes describing their inputs, outputs, assumptions, and failure cases.

sessions.name: Session 4: Logic, Branches, and Room Decisions
sessions.objectives: Design decision rules that connect colony state to actions under changing resource and room conditions.
sessions.liveActivity: Build decision flows for empty creep storage, full creep storage, available energy, spawn capacity, controller upgrades, and immediate room priorities.
sessions.homework: Add one Screeps decision rule and document the signal it reads, the action it triggers, and what can go wrong.

sessions.name: Session 5: Git Checkpoints for a Living Bot
sessions.objectives: Use Git to protect system experiments, compare strategy changes, and recover from broken colony behavior.
sessions.liveActivity: Initialize or update the Screeps bot repo, make clean commits, review diffs, and connect version control to design decisions.
sessions.homework: Push the Screeps repo and write a README section explaining the current colony architecture.

sessions.name: Session 6: Debugging a Running Colony
sessions.objectives: Diagnose system behavior by inspecting errors, logs, stuck creeps, idle spawns, broken memory, and unclear AI suggestions before changing code.
sessions.liveActivity: Diagnose real Screeps failures and practice turning room observations into precise Codex prompts.
sessions.homework: Open one repo issue describing a Screeps bug, the evidence, and the next fix to try.

sessions.name: Session 7: APIs, Game Objects, and Interfaces
sessions.objectives: Read Screeps game objects, method calls, JSON-like data, and the interface between your code and the game world.
sessions.liveActivity: Inspect creep, room, source, controller, and spawn objects, then model how external APIs use similar request and response patterns.
sessions.homework: Document one Screeps object or method as if it were an API: what it receives, returns, and changes.

sessions.name: Session 8: Memory, Data Flow, and Roles
sessions.objectives: Organize data so the colony can track roles, tasks, energy needs, room priorities, and strategy over time.
sessions.liveActivity: Design Memory structures and connect harvester, upgrader, builder, and defender roles to the behavior components and rules already built.
sessions.homework: Add or revise one Screeps Memory structure and explain why it helps the colony scale.

sessions.name: Session 9: Automation Loops and Scaling
sessions.objectives: Design automation loops that use triggers, rules, and repeated actions to keep the colony moving without manual intervention.
sessions.liveActivity: Build spawn automation, role balancing, repair/build priorities, and fallback behavior while discussing where automation helps and fails.
sessions.homework: Add one Screeps automation rule and document its trigger, expected effect, and failure mode.

sessions.name: Session 10: Codex as a Screeps Build Partner
sessions.objectives: Use Codex to pressure-test architecture, plan Screeps features, explain errors, and review work while keeping builder judgment first.
sessions.liveActivity: Run a guided Codex workflow: describe a colony improvement, inspect the generated code, test in Screeps, and commit only work you can explain.
sessions.homework: Use Codex for one Screeps improvement and write what changed, what was accepted, and what was rejected.

sessions.name: Session 11: Tournament Week: Battle Branch and CTF Prep
sessions.objectives: Turn the colony architecture into a battle-ready branch for AutoNateAI capture-the-flag rules.
sessions.liveActivity: Review the colony mechanics, identify attack and defense priorities, tune the tournament branch, and decide what the bot should protect, chase, ignore, or optimize.
sessions.homework: Finish the tournament branch and write a short strategy note explaining how your colony plans to win.

sessions.name: Session 12: Tournament Week: Capture-the-Flag Battles
sessions.objectives: Battle colonies head-to-head in AutoNateAI capture-the-flag, explain the architecture, review tradeoffs, and identify the next version for league-style improvement.
sessions.liveActivity: Participants submit tournament branches, run Screeps capture-the-flag matches, watch code-driven colony strategies collide, and analyze how mechanics, state, automation, and architecture shaped the outcome.
sessions.homework: Submit final repo link, Screeps match screenshots or clips, architecture notes, tournament reflection, and next-build plan.

offerings.name: AI Systems Programming Lab - August Cohort
offerings.meetingFrequency: 6 weeks, Tuesdays and Thursdays, 6:30 PM-8:30 PM Eastern

product.title: AutoNateAI Tournament Day
product.cta: The final week is AutoNateAI tournament week: builders tune battle branches, then play capture-the-flag inside Screeps where every move, defense, and resource decision comes from code.

season.name: August Cohort Tournament Day
season.winCondition: Participants submit battle-ready Screeps bot branches to the tournament server. Colonies go head-to-head under capture-the-flag rules while builders explain how their environment reading, component design, Git history, automation decisions, and strategy shaped the match.
